content:
Optimization opportunity for pointers to aligned objects

Consider the following Rust (Linux kernel) code:

enum Node {
    Pointer(KBox<Node>),
    Internal(u8),
    Value(u8),
}

The size of Node is 2 words, one for the discriminant and one for the value.
However, because Node has an alignment of at least 4 bytes, the discriminant could
be encoded in the lower two bits of the first word.

This is an optimization that is applied by hand in key areas of the Linux kernel
C code to achieve lower memory pressure and better cache utilization.
Specifically, the Linux kernel's C xarray uses this pattern.

For the Linux kernel's XArray, this means that an idiomatic Rust implementation
uses twice the memory of the C implementation. Matching the memory density of
the C implementation with Rust code requires manual pointer tagging with unsafe
code.

The effort to implement the Linux kernel xarray in Rust is driven in part by
memory safety issues encountered in the existing C implementation. Thus, having
to reach for unsafe blocks for cases like this is very undesirable.

Use Case: Linux Kernel XArray

The XArray is a kernel data structure that acts as a key/value map, mapping integers
to object addresses, or integers to integers. It's used in the page cache,
filesystems, and block layer.

Pointers stored in XArray must be 4-byte aligned (true for kmalloc() and
alloc_page() returns). Integers between 0 and LONG_MAX can be stored via
xa_mk_value().

The XArray is a radix tree with a default shift value of 6 such that each node
stores 64 pointers to the next level of the tree. In C, with 8-byte tagged
pointers, this results in 512-byte nodes. An idiomatic Rust implementation
requires 16 bytes per entry (8 for discriminant + 8 for value), resulting in
1024-byte nodes. The performance impact of this difference is significant.

Conclusion

There exists an optimization opportunity for Rust enums that contain pointers to
aligned objects. Unlocking this optimization is key in achieving a performant
Rust XArray implementation without unsafe blocks.

@team @Ding Xiang Fei @Daniel Gomez
Linked to https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/354.
I think you can just apply the conversion on the API boundaries in this case.
Having 0..PAGE_SIZE be a niche is sufficient for this one.
I think it won't be enough if you change u8 to u16
Even if rust does support niche optimisation for this one, you still cannot assume on the layout though, so I don't see much benefit for XArray.

use std::mem::{self, ManuallyDrop};
use std::ptr;

// Assuming KBox is an alias for Box or a similar kernel smart pointer.
// We use Box here for demonstration.
pub type KBox<T> = Box<T>;

#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct PackedNode {
    ptr: *const Node,
}

pub enum Node {
    Pointer(KBox<Node>),
    Internal(u8),
    Value(u8),
}

impl PackedNode {
    const TAG_MASK: usize = 0b11;
    const TAG_PTR: usize = 0b00;
    const TAG_INT: usize = 0b01;
    const TAG_VAL: usize = 0b10;

    pub fn new_pointer(kbox: KBox<Node>) -> Self {
        let ptr = KBox::into_raw(kbox) as *const Node;
        debug_assert_eq!(ptr.addr() & Self::TAG_MASK, Self::TAG_PTR);
        Self { ptr }
    }

    pub fn new_internal(val: u8) -> Self {
        let addr = ((val as usize) << 2) | Self::TAG_INT;
        let ptr = ptr::without_provenance(addr);
        Self { ptr }
    }

    pub fn new_value(val: u8) -> Self {
        let addr = ((val as usize) << 2) | Self::TAG_VAL;
        let ptr = ptr::without_provenance(addr);
        Self { ptr }
    }

    pub fn unpack(self) -> Node {
        let this = ManuallyDrop::new(self);
        let ptr = this.ptr;
        let tag = ptr.addr() & Self::TAG_MASK;

        match tag {
            Self::TAG_PTR => unsafe {
                let kbox = KBox::from_raw(ptr as *mut Node);
                Node::Pointer(kbox)
            },
            Self::TAG_INT => {
                let val = (addr >> 2) as u8;
                Node::Internal(val)
            }
            Self::TAG_VAL => {
                let val = (addr >> 2) as u8;
                Node::Value(val)
            }
            _ => unreachable!(),
        }
    }

    pub fn is_pointer(&self) -> bool {
        (self.ptr.addr() & Self::TAG_MASK) == Self::TAG_PTR
    }
}

// Drop implementation is critical to prevent memory leaks for the KBox variant.
impl Drop for Node {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if self.is_pointer() {
            unsafe { drop(KBox::from_raw(self.ptr as *mut Node)) };
        }
    }
}

unsafe impl Send for PackedNode where Node: Send {}
unsafe impl Sync for PackedNode where Node: Sync {}

Alice Ryhl said:

use std::mem::{self, ManuallyDrop};
use std::ptr;

// Assuming KBox is an alias for Box or a similar kernel smart pointer.
// We use Box here for demonstration.
pub type KBox<T> = Box<T>;

#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct PackedNode {
    ptr: *const Node,
}

pub enum Node {
    Pointer(KBox<Node>),
    Internal(u8),
    Value(u8),
}

impl PackedNode {
    const TAG_MASK: usize = 0b11;
    const TAG_PTR: usize = 0b00;
    const TAG_INT: usize = 0b01;
    const TAG_VAL: usize = 0b10;

    pub fn new_pointer(kbox: KBox<Node>) -> Self {
        let ptr = KBox::into_raw(kbox) as *const Node;
        debug_assert_eq!(ptr.addr() & Self::TAG_MASK, Self::TAG_PTR);
        Self { ptr }
    }

    pub fn new_internal(val: u8) -> Self {
        let addr = ((val as usize) << 2) | Self::TAG_INT;
        let ptr = ptr::without_provenance(addr);
        Self { ptr }
    }

    pub fn new_value(val: u8) -> Self {
        let addr = ((val as usize) << 2) | Self::TAG_VAL;
        let ptr = ptr::without_provenance(addr);
        Self { ptr }
    }

    pub fn unpack(self) -> Node {
        let this = ManuallyDrop::new(self);
        let ptr = this.ptr;
        let tag = ptr.addr() & Self::TAG_MASK;

        match tag {
            Self::TAG_PTR => unsafe {
                let kbox = KBox::from_raw(ptr as *mut Node);
                Node::Pointer(kbox)
            },
            Self::TAG_INT => {
                let val = (addr >> 2) as u8;
                Node::Internal(val)
            }
            Self::TAG_VAL => {
                let val = (addr >> 2) as u8;
                Node::Value(val)
            }
            _ => unreachable!(),
        }
    }

    pub fn is_pointer(&self) -> bool {
        (self.ptr.addr() & Self::TAG_MASK) == Self::TAG_PTR
    }
}

// Drop implementation is critical to prevent memory leaks for the KBox variant.
impl Drop for Node {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if self.is_pointer() {
            unsafe { drop(KBox::from_raw(self.ptr as *mut Node)) };
        }
    }
}

unsafe impl Send for PackedNode where Node: Send {}
unsafe impl Sync for PackedNode where Node: Sync {}

This is what we do now. We think that the compiler should be able to do this for us.

Gary Guo said:

I think you can just apply the conversion on the API boundaries in this case.

This is what we do. The request is to have the compiler do it for us, so we don't need to do a manual conversion.

Gary Guo said:

Even if rust does support niche optimisation for this one, you still cannot assume on the layout though, so I don't see much benefit for XArray.

If we had a way to restrict the object stored in the pointer, we could benefit.
I don't think this is something that should be done by the language or compiler

Gary Guo said:

I don't think this is something that should be done by the language or compiler

Why not?
It's a huge complication to the language with very little gain, given that implementing it with library is already pretty easy
do you have a branch with the code?
Will there ever be any possibility to implement from library to use the niche of a pointer value for enum discriminant, if the enum is #[repr(Rust)]?

I also see a good use of it when pattern types eventually land. A natural question that comes right after is, well, if it is now possible to have bit_pattern!(u64, 0xfffffffffffff800), can I use the unused niche below those bits, given that one knows already u64: Copy?
Doing Niche optimization is fine, but I don't think there's should be any guarantee on the exact optimization being performed
For embedded and environments that sizes matter, they are happy with possible layout optimisation. It will be good enough for them.

Benno Lossin said:

do you have a branch with the code?

Not at the moment, but we will be making the code public soon :tm:

Ding Xiang Fei said:

For embedded and environments that sizes matter, they are happy with possible layout optimisation. It will be good enough for them.

We would defninitly need to be able to rely on the optimization for the XArray code, in the same way I expect Option<NonNull<_>> to be representable in one word now.
Without triggering the optimization reliably, we would have to use manual conversion anyway.

Alice Ryhl said:

Having 0..PAGE_SIZE be a niche is sufficient for this one.

The values able to be stored in C xarray is actually 0..(2^(64-2)-1). I just illustrated with u8.
Can I do an integer with a custom range in Rust?

Andreas Hindborg said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Having 0..PAGE_SIZE be a niche is sufficient for this one.

The values able to be stored in C xarray is actually 0..(2^(64-2)-1). I just illustrated with u8.

XArray can store anything that is 4-byte aligned. See paragraph 3 in docs:
https://docs.kernel.org/core-api/xarray.html

Daniel Gomez said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Having 0..PAGE_SIZE be a niche is sufficient for this one.

The values able to be stored in C xarray is actually 0..(2^(64-2)-1). I just illustrated with u8.

XArray can store anything that is 4-byte aligned. See paragraph 3 in docs:
https://docs.kernel.org/core-api/xarray.html

I mean if you store integers rather than pointers. The API takes an unsigned long and shifts it. It is actualy just a 1 bit shift, so the range is 0..(2^(64-1)-1):

static inline void *xa_mk_value(unsigned long v)
{
    WARN_ON((long)v < 0);
    return (void *)((v << 1) | 1);
}

Andreas Hindborg said:

Can I do an integer with a custom range in Rust?

no, though there are proposals
I just did not have a way to express that in rust.

Benno Lossin said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Can I do an integer with a custom range in Rust?

no, though there are proposals

How about the niche optimization of values like Option<NonNull<_>>? Is the optimization guaranteed or is it best effort?
For the specific case of Option wrapping a pointer, it's guaranteed.
But it's the only guaranteed case.

Alice Ryhl said:

But it's the only guaranteed case.

Is this compiler internal tribal knowledge, or can I read about this somewhere?
https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/option/index.html#representation

@Gary Guo

Andreas Hindborg said:

Daniel Gomez said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Having 0..PAGE_SIZE be a niche is sufficient for this one.

The values able to be stored in C xarray is actually 0..(2^(64-2)-1). I just illustrated with u8.

XArray can store anything that is 4-byte aligned. See paragraph 3 in docs:
https://docs.kernel.org/core-api/xarray.html

I mean if you store integers rather than pointers. The API takes an unsigned long and shifts it. It is actualy just a 1 bit shift, so the range is 0..(2^(64-1)-1):

static inline void *xa_mk_value(unsigned long v)
{
    WARN_ON((long)v < 0);
    return (void *)((v << 1) | 1);
}

Agree. We do have usize::MAX >> 1 as max value that can be stored.
@Daniel Gomez found RFC 3334. It is somehow absent from https://rust-lang.github.io/rfcs/. It seems relevant.
@Daniel Gomez I feel like I didn't understand your last point in the meeting, do you mind spelling it out again here?

Benno Lossin said:

Daniel Gomez I feel like I didn't understand your last point in the meeting, do you mind spelling it out again here?

Just a follow up on the idea to have a separate macro to handle niches and focus on the implementation. IMO, this doesn't "solve" the problem that @Andreas Hindborg pointed out at the beginning where having unsafe for dealing with these things, defeats the purpose of writing this data structure in Rust when one of the reasons is to have memory safety. This is especially true when this macro would be used in the hot path.

I think Dani was saying that even if we move the unsafe code out of the data structure implementation, if it is still in the hot path of page management, it will have a very high execution count. So moving the unsafe code out is no help.

I don' t agree in this observation (correct me if I'm wrong about the interpretation Dani) for a few reasons. First, I don't think it matters too much if the code is in hot path or not. If it is flawed, it might be exploitable no matter the placement.

Second, the code will be in the hot path no matter if we write it or if the compiler writes it. We just want the compiler to write it for us. The code will be more or less the same.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think Dani was saying that even if we move the unsafe code out of the data structure implementation, if it is still in the hot path of page management, it will have a very high execution count. So moving the unsafe code out is no help.

I don' t agree in this observation (correct me if I'm wrong about the interpretation Dani) for a few reasons. First, I don't think it matters too much if the code is in hot path or not. If it is flawed, it might be exploitable no matter the placement.

Second, the code will be in the hot path no matter if we write it or if the compiler writes it. We just want the compiler to write it for us. The code will be more or less the same.

Agree on both. On the first part, I guess I though it was even more critical when code is in the hot path. If not, that's good too.
are you saying that the manual code is not optimal and the compiler would do better?

Daniel Gomez said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think Dani was saying that even if we move the unsafe code out of the data structure implementation, if it is still in the hot path of page management, it will have a very high execution count. So moving the unsafe code out is no help.

I don' t agree in this observation (correct me if I'm wrong about the interpretation Dani) for a few reasons. First, I don't think it matters too much if the code is in hot path or not. If it is flawed, it might be exploitable no matter the placement.

Second, the code will be in the hot path no matter if we write it or if the compiler writes it. We just want the compiler to write it for us. The code will be more or less the same.

Agree on both. On the first part, I guess I though it was even more critical when code is in the hot path. If not, that's good too.

I guess it is debatable. If you have a vulnerability it may be easier to trigger if it is in the hot path. Or it might not be. I don't think it is black and white.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Daniel Gomez said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think Dani was saying that even if we move the unsafe code out of the data structure implementation, if it is still in the hot path of page management, it will have a very high execution count. So moving the unsafe code out is no help.

I don' t agree in this observation (correct me if I'm wrong about the interpretation Dani) for a few reasons. First, I don't think it matters too much if the code is in hot path or not. If it is flawed, it might be exploitable no matter the placement.

Second, the code will be in the hot path no matter if we write it or if the compiler writes it. We just want the compiler to write it for us. The code will be more or less the same.

Agree on both. On the first part, I guess I though it was even more critical when code is in the hot path. If not, that's good too.

I guess it is debatable. If you have a vulnerability it may be easier to trigger if it is in the hot path. Or it might not be. I don't think it is black and white.

I guess it'd be worse when that macro is flawed/exploitable and have multiple users.

because if the problem really is only the unsafe code, then I don't agree with the argument. All Rust code ultimately relies on unsafe code being correct, most rust drivers will be using pin-init, which generates unsafe code, but we don't distrust code using it
moving unsafe code to the compiler also isn't 100% foolproof, the compiler could have a bug

Benno Lossin said:

are you saying that the manual code is not optimal and the compiler would do better?

I think the reasoning is that unsafe blocks yields options to make mistakes. The thing is, the compiler is one big unsafe block. I don't think it matters much whether the compiler does this for us, or if we do it by hand. Both need to be right. But stuff is not correct, just because it is in the compiler.
I'm not saying the compiler should not do this for us. Eventually it should. But having a hand coded thing for now is not the end of the world.
I think we should do the macro library before you write an RFC
because that can just live in rust/macros or a new crate

Benno Lossin said:

I think we should do the macro library before you write an RFC

Then we could also allow the use of the unstable features only in this crate, right? Otherwise it would be enabled in all of kernel?
which unstable feature do we need? Alice Ryhl's suggestion above works on stable IIRC
If we want to play around with rustc_layout_scalar_valid_range_start or pattern types, when they appear.
We already implemented a solution similar to what Alice suggested. Neither ours or Alice is generic enough to put it in an external crate though.
But we can probably write something generic with a macro.
I'm saying it's not difficult to take Alice's code and make it the output of a macro
I'm not sure you even need a macro. We can make a generic container over any ForeignOwnable

Benno Lossin said:

I'm saying it's not difficult to take Alice's code and make it the output of a macro

Right, I agree. That is probably the shortest path to chart.

Alice Ryhl said:

I'm not sure you even need a macro. We can make a generic container over any ForeignOwnable

Maybe. I'd like to be able to adjust the alignment constraint as well, but I guess that can be done.

Alice Ryhl said:

I'm not sure you even need a macro. We can make a generic container over any ForeignOwnable

I don't understand how that would look like though

A quick sketch:

enum Unpacked<T: ForeignOwnable> {
    Pointer(T),
    Tagged {
        tag: Bounded<NonNull<usize>, {align_to_bits(T::FOREIGN_ALIGN)}>,
        value: Bounded<usize, {usize::BITS-align_to_bits(T::FOREIGN_ALIGN)}>,
    },
}

struct Packed<T: ForeignOwnable> {
    packed: *const c_void,
}
Does Bounded exist?
Yes, see rust/kernel/num/bounded.rs
though I don't think it supports using NonNull<usize> as the underlying integer type right now - but it could be added
Ok, cool. We can give that a try. I am curious about how the generated code will be.

Though it probably has to look like this to avoid needing unstable features for const generics:

enum Unpacked<T: ForeignOwnable, const N: usize, const M: usize> {
    Pointer(T),
    Tagged {
        tag: Bounded<NonNull<usize>, N>,
        value: Bounded<usize, M>,
    },
}

// inside conversion from Unpacked to Packed:
const {
    assert!((1 << N) <= T::FOREIGN_ALIGN);
    assert!(N + M <= usize::BITS);
}

I think the macro approach is much more ergonomic, you just write

#[packed_pointer]
pub enum Node {
    Pointer(KBox<Node>),
    Internal(u8),
    Value(u8),
}

And then you get a PackedNode type that has all the constructors you need
I think my approach is easier to validate the unsafe code of. One can have a macro that outputs fully safe code to convert between the two versions of the unpacked struct.
by that is a matter of taste
right that's also a good option for doing things
AFAIK const generics is also progressing a lot, so it's only a matter of time until we can get some of those nice improvements
